A 56-year-old woman with a 10-year history of type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypertension is referred to you for evaluation of hypercalcemia. Other notable medical history includes hyperlipidemia diagnosed 7 years ago and bipolar disorder diagnosed 5 years ago. Over the last year, she reports worsening fatigue, depression, anxiety, and myalgias. As a result, her primary care physician initiated a workup for other potential problems since her medical status has been stable. A screening chemistry panel documented hypercalcemia, which prompted further workup, the results of which are shown below. Laboratory test results: Serum calcium = 11.5 mg/dL (2.88 mmol/L) Serum albumin = 3.7 g/dL (37 g/L) Serum creatinine = 0.55 mg/dL (48.6 μmol/L) $$ \mathrm{PTH}=108\mathrm{pg}/\mathrm{mL}(108\mathrm{ng}/\mathrm{L}) $$ Estimated glomerular filtration rate => 90 mL/min
A parathyroid scan shows the presence of activity in 2 parathyroid glands (see image). Bone mineral density testing reveals the following T scores: spine, -2.1; left femoral neck, -2.4; and distal radius, -2.9. In her medical records, you observe numerous chemistry panels obtained over the years, and calcium levels have always been normal, including a measurement 2 years ago. She has never had a kidney stone or fracture. She has never taken lithium. There is no family history of calcium disorders. Current medications include liraglutide injections, simvastatin, nebivolol, and ziprasidone. On physical examination, blood pressure is 142/88 mm Hg. Height is 64 in (162.6 cm), and weight is 235 lb (106.8 kg) (BMI = 40.3 kg/m $ ^{2} $). Otherwise, findings from the rest of her examination (including the head and neck) are normal. You conclude that she has primary hyperparathyroidism and that she is an appropriate surgical candidate. Which of the following is most likely to improve in this patient after successful 2-gland parathyroidectomy?
items.explanation: Correct answer: A.

A 50-year-old woman with a history of type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, lactose intolerance, andột mối obesity is referred to you for evaluation of hyperparathyroidism and bone loss. Roux-en-Y bariatric surgery was performed 2 years ago. She has successfully lost nearly 100 lb (45.5 kg), and her diabetes is now well controlled off medication. She currently weighs 225 lb (102.4 kg), and height is 65 in (165.1 cm) (BMI = 37.4 kg/m²). Since her surgery, she has taken 4000 IU of vitamin D₃ daily and metoprolol for hypertension.

She is seen every 6 months for routine follow-up. DXA was performed last year, as well as at her most recent visit, to evaluate bone mineral density. Results from her current DXA scan reveal a lumbar spine T score of -2.2 (representing a 5.7% decline from the previous study) and a femoral neck T score of -2.7 (representing a 2.5% decline from the previous study). As a result, her primary care physician initiated an evaluation for the cause of bone loss.

Laboratory test results:

 $$  Serum~calcium=8.4mg/dL(2.10mmol/L) $$ 

 $$ \mathrm{Albumin}=4.0\mathrm{~g/dL}\left(40\mathrm{~g/L}\right) $$ 

 $$ \mathrm{PTH}=141\mathrm{pg}/\mathrm{mL}(141\mathrm{ng}/\mathrm{L}) $$ 

 $$ 25-Hydroxyvitamin~D=66ng/mL(164.7nmol/L) $$ 

 $$ \mathrm{U r i n a r y c a l c i u m}=19\mathrm{m g}/24\mathrm{h}(0.48\mathrm{m m o l/d}) $$ 

 $$ (s i m u l t a n e o u s c r e a t i n i n e o f0.99g/24h[8.75m m o l/d]) $$ 

 $$ \mathrm{Serum~phosphorus}=2.0\mathrm{mg/dL}(0.65\mathrm{mmol/L}) $$ 

In addition to continuing her current medications and nutritional supplements, which of the following is the best therapeutic intervention?
options: Elemental calcium, 2000 mg daily, and ergocalciferol, 50,000 IU weekly
Elemental calcium, 2000 mg daily, and alendronate, 70 mg weekly
Elemental calcium, 2000 mg daily, and cinacalcet, 30 mg daily
items.explanation:
## 29 ANSWER: A) Elemental calcium, 2000 mg daily

29 After bariatric surgery, the loss of bone mineral density documented on DXA scan is relatively common. In patient, some of the bone loss seen on DXA is artifact due to extreme changes in fat mass. DXA measurement is limited in the obese population because of the inability to discriminate between cortical and trabecular bone. Indeed studies done with quantitative CT tend to reveal stable bone mass, suggesting that the DXA results may be artifact. However, markers of bone turnover (both formation and resorption) increase markedly after weight-reduction surge and hence the potential for clinically significant bone loss is a possible adverse outcome with these procedures.

Bone loss associated with bariatric surgery is multifactorial. There is a strong association between the low weight loss and loss of bone mineral density in weight-bearing sites including the hip and femoral neck, while is postulated to be due to adaptive changes in skeletal unloading. At baseline, morbidly obese patients have low vitamin D levels because of reduced bioavailability from sequestration into fat tissue. Malabsorption of vitamin D is also found in patients with other nutrients, after bariatric surgery is well recognized. Vitamin D dosages needed to restore normal levels after bariatric surgery can be extremely large, perhaps up to 15,000 or 20,000 IU daily. Ergocalciferol (vitamin D₃) comes in a 50,000 IU preparation and is often used for high-dosage replacement.

This patient has secondary hyperparathyroidism with a normal serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D level. Thus, additional vitamin D (Answers B) is not needed. However, the urinary calcium excretion is exceedingly low and the secondary hyperparathyroidism is thus due to calcium deficiency. Calcium transporters are most prevalent in the duodenum; this is the area of best absorption of both dietary calcium and supplements. After Roux-en-Y bariatric surgery, the duodenum is bypassed. Current guidelines recommend up to 1500 mg of calcium daily a minimum of 3000 IU of vitamin D daily to avoid bone loss. In this patient with declining bone density and calmalabsorption, a higher dosage of calcium such as 2000 mg daily or more, may be needed (Answer A is correct).

Secondary hyperparathyroidism would not be treated with parathyroidectomy. PTH rise is in response to the calcium deficiency, not the primary problem. Calcium receptor mimetics such as cinacalcet can reduce PTH levels but do not address the inherent process causing the problem. Indeed, calcium receptor mimetics reduce calcium levels and could cause clinical hypocalcemia in this patient with circulating calcium levels already at the lower end of the normal range (thus, Answer D is incorrect).

One should not institute antiresorptive therapy with alendronate (Answer C) or any other bisphosphonate the presence of either calcium or vitamin D deficiency.

##### Educational Objective:

Identify calcium deficiency seen in the setting of obesity and bariatric surgery as a common cause of secondary hyperparathyroidism.

UpToDate Topic Review(s):

Bariatric surgery: Postoperative nutritional management

##### Reference(s):

Hamoui N, Anthone G, Crookes PF. Calcium metabolism in the morbidly obese. Obes Surg. 2004:14(1):9-12.

Sánchez-Hernández J, Ybarra J, Olчи I, et al. Effects of bariatric surgery on vitamin D status and secondary hyperparathyroidism: a prospective study. 2005;15(10):1389-1395.

Yu EW, Bouxsein M, Roy AE, et al. Bone loss after bariatric surgery: discordant results between DXA and QCT bone density. J Bone Miner Res. 2014;29(3):542-550.

items.question:
A 44-year-old man presents to your office for a second opinion regarding low bone density and possible hypogonadism. He recently sustained a T12 spinal compression fracture while stepping off a curb, and low bone density has been confirmed on DXA (both lumbar spine and femoral neck T scores are less than -2.5). He has a history of hypothyroidism treated with levothyroxine. He notes increasing fatigue over the past several years, along with decreased libido and decreased early morning erections.

On physical examination, weight is 185 lb (84.1 kg) and height is 70 in (177.8 cm) (BMI = 26.5 kg/m²). Blood pressure is 134/78 mm Hg, and heart rate is 82 beats/min. He has no kyphosis, but he does have tenderness to spinal palpation near the T12 vertebra. His testicular size is within normal limits but the testes are soft. There is no synovitis or joint swelling. Reflexes are normal, as are the rest of the examination findings.

Laboratory test results (sample drawn while fasting):

Hemoglobin = 14 g/dL (140 g/L)

TSH = 3.2 mIU/L

Free  $ T_4 = 0.9 $ ng/dL (11.6 pmol/L)

FSH = 3.2 mIU/mL (3.2 IU/L)

LH = 2.1 mIU/mL (2.1 IU/L)

25-Hydroxyvitamin D = 34 ng/mL (84.9 nmol/L)

Intact PTH = 42 pg/mL (42 ng/L)

Calcium = 9.4 mg/dL (2.4 mmol/L)

Serum glucose = 176 mg/dL (9.8 mmol/L)

Total testosterone (8 AM) = 180 ng/dL (6.1 nmol/L)

Which of the following is the best next step?
options: Measure transferrin saturation and ferritin concentration
items.explanation:
## 36 ANSWER: C) Measure transferrin saturation and ferritin concentration

36 ANSWER: Men with osteoporosis should be evaluated for secondary causes because in almost half of cases, a modern condition causing bone loss is identified. Although this patient reports fatigue and has a history of hypothyroidism, the is on levothyroxine replacement, and his serum TSH and free T₄ concentrations are normal. In patients with hemochromatosis (as is the case here), secondary hypothyroidism is possible and the TSH level may be misleadingly low. With a normal free T₄ level, the diagnosis of secondary hypothyroidism is excluded. Moreover, secondary hypothyroidism is uncommon and without signs of hypothyroidism on examination (bradycardia, delayed relaxation phase of reflexes, etc), this diagnosis would be highly unlikely. Measuring free T₄ levels (Answer A) would probably not provide additional diagnostic information and would not explain osteoporosis, hypogonadism, and new-onset diabetes. Measuring hemoglobin A₁c (Answer B) would better characterize his diabetes control, but would not provide a unifying diagnosis. Likewise, measuring free testosterone (Answer D) would not be appropriate to evaluate for an adenoma, mass, and infiltrative causes of central hypogonadism with resultant osteoporosis. Involvement of only the pituitary would not explain the new-onset diabetes.

Hemochromatosis is a hereditary disorder that results in iron deposition in multiple organs, including the endocrine glands. Diabetes mellitus, both primary and secondary hypogonadism, secondary hypothyroidism, and hepatic dysfunction can all result from iron overload. In fact, this patient has an elevated fasting glucose concentration, consistent with β-cell destruction by iron overload. Iron accumulation also occurs in the pituitary, which causes secondary hypogonadism, reflected by low FSH and LH levels, as observed in this patient. Additionally, there are reported cases of testicular failure due to gonadal iron deposition. The hypogonadism results from hemochromatosis most likely contributes to osteoporosis, and this patient should receive testosterone replacement, at least until testicular function may be restored. Depending on the severity and duration of untreated hemochromatosis, pituitary function may return with iron-depletion strategies. Interestingly, the rates of osteoporosis and fractures are higher than would be predicted by hypogonadism alone, and can even occur in the absence of hypogonadism. Patients with hemochromatosis have a higher incidence of osteopenia and osteoporosis as well as an increased fracture rate. Phlebotomy and reduction of iron accumulation can improve bone mineral density, and proper diagnosis is crucial. A fasting transferrin saturation and ferritin concentration (Answer C) should be assessed.

##### Educational Objective:

Evaluate for hemochromatosis in a patient with osteoporosis in the setting of hypogonadism and diabetes mellitus

UpToDate Topic Review(s):

Clinical manifestations of hereditary hemochromatosis

##### Reference(s):

Valenti L, Varenna M, Fracanzani AL, Rossi V, Fargion S, Sinigaglia L. Association between iron overload and osteoporosis in patients with hereditary hemochromatosis. Osteoporos Int. 2009;20(4):549-555.

Guggenbuhl P, Brissot P, Loréal O. Miscellaneous non-inflammatory musculoskeletal conditions. Haemochromatosis: the bone and the joint. Best Pract Res Clin Rheumatol. 2011;25(5):649-664.

Sinigaglia L, Fargion S, Fracanzani AL, et al. Bone and joint involvement in genetic hemochromatosis: role of cirrhosis and iron overload. J Rheumatol. 1997;24(9):1809-1813.

Guggenbuhl P, Deugnier Y, Boisdet JF, et al. Bone mineral density in men with genetic hemochromatosis and HFE gene mutation. Osteoporos Int. 2005;16(12):1809-1814.

items.question:
A 38-year-old man is referred to you for hyperparathyroidism that has persisted after parathyroidectomy. He initially underwent evaluation 8 months ago when he was determined to have asymptomatic hypercalcemia. He had not seen a physician in many years, so there was no previous calcium value for comparison. He has no history of peptic ulcer disease, nephrolithiasis, or hypertension. The physician caring for him 8 months ago documented normal findings on physical examination and ordered the following laboratory workup:

Serum calcium = 11.5 mg/dL (2.88 mmol/L)

25-Hydroxyvitamin D = 22 ng/mL (54.9 nmol/L)

 $$ \mathrm{PTH}=50\mathrm{pg}/\mathrm{mL}(50\mathrm{ng}/\mathrm{L}) $$ 

Serum phosphorus = 2.1 mg/dL (0.68 mmol/L)

Serum creatinine = 1.05 mg/dL (92.8 μmol/L)

Neck ultrasonography was performed, but this failed to identify an enlarged parathyroid gland.

Given his age and the degree of hypercalcemia, he was deemed to be a candidate for parathyroidectomy. Two enlarged glands were resected during the operation, with the final pathology report documenting hyperplasia in both glands. In addition, intraoperative PTH levels remained elevated. Postoperatively, his calcium concentration was 11.6 mg/dL (2.90 mmol/L). Hence, he is sent to you for further treatment.

On physical examination, he is a well-nourished man. Height is 66.5 in (168.9 cm), and weight is 242 lb (110 kg) (BMI = 38.5 kg/m²). Blood pressure is 145/98 mm Hg, and pulse rate is 91 beats/min. Neck examination reveals a normal thyroid gland without palpable masses. His parathyroidectomy scar is healing well and the rest of his examination findings are normal.

On repeated testing, you confirm the hypercalcemia and the PTH concentration in the upper-normal range.

Which of the following is the best next step in his care?
options: Perform a second surgery with biopsy and resection of 1 or both remaining parathyroid glands D.Order bone densitometry
Measure 24-hour urinary calcium and creatinine excretion

items.question:
You are asked to see a 62-year-old woman who had a decrease in bone mineral density documented on her next step in her care. most recent DXA scan. She had been doing well, but the decline is a concern and she wants to discuss the next step in her care. An initial DXA was done at age 56 years, and she had 2 subsequent follow-up studies (at 60 and 62 years). After her first DXA scan, she was prescribed calcium, 1000 mg daily; vitamin D₃, 1000 IU daily; and alendronate, 70 mg weekly. After 4 years of treatment, and at the time of the second DXA scan, the alendronate was discontinued. She has continued to take calcium and vitamin D. She takes no prescription medications. She is active and has no notable medical history. Findings on physical examination are unremarkable. A chemistry panel is normal and her 25-hydroxyvitamin D level is 35 ng/mL (87.4 nmol/L). The DXA scan from age 60 years shows a lumbar spine T score of -1.7 and a femoral neck T score of -2.2. The DXA scan from age 62 years shows a lumbar spine T score of -1.4 and a femoral neck T score of -3.0.
Age, yLumbar Spine ImageHip Image

Which of the following is the best next step?
items.explanation:
## 54 ANSWER: E) Perform another DXA scan 54 The DXA scan is an essential tool used in the evaluation and follow-up of patients with low bone density and osteoporosis. Measurement of bone density is the best correlate with fracture risk and it is an integral part of the FRAX analysis for long-term risk assessment. However, DXA is not without potential error. Proper and consistent positioning of the patient, in particular for measurement of the femoral neck and hip, is critical. If one examines the 2 sets of images presented in the vignette, a clear difference in technique can be observed. The DXA from 2 years earlier (along with the first and second studies in the sequence) includes some portion of the pelvis in the measurement of the femoral neck (see arrow).
In the most recent study, the measured region does not include that bone mineral and hence the conclusion when comparing the 2 studies is that there has been a significant decline. This patient had another DXA study performed (Answer E). Her bone density was lower when compared with her bone density from the scan 2 years ago, but by less than 1%. Further observation was elected as management and no additional diagnostic information was needed. Thus, whenever there is an unexpected change in bone density, one must examine the images to ensure that the studies can be legitimately compared. Osteocalcin (Answer A, and N-telopeptide (Answer B) are markers of bone turnover (formation and resorption, respectively) and their measurement is not needed in this vignette. Nothing about this patient's physical examination findings suggests hypercortisolism and there is a better explanation for the decline in bone density than the development of Cushing syndrome (thus, Answer C is incorrect). Bone biopsy (Answer D) is rarely, if ever, helpful in such patients. ##### Educational Objective: Interpret results from dual-energy x-ray absorptiometry scanning and describe the limitations and potential errors of this tool. UpToDate Topic Review(s): Screening for osteoporosis ##### Reference(s): Conference on bone densitometry. J Clin Densitom. 2013;16(4):455-466.

items.question:
A 23-year-old woman presents to establish endocrine care for longstanding hypoparathyroidism caused by autosomal dominant hypocalcemia. Her medications consist of oral calcium supplements (calcium carbonate), 2500 mg daily in divided doses, and calcitriol, 0.5 mcg twice daily. She is adherent with her medication regimen, yet she occasionally develops symptomatic hypocalcemia with paresthesias and muscle cramps.

On physical examination, weight is 131 lb (59.5 kg) and height is 65 in (165.1 cm) (BMI = 21.8 kg/m²). Blood pressure is 108/74 mm Hg, and heart rate is 70 beats/min. Her reflexes are normal, and she does not have evidence of the Chvostek or Trousseau sign. The rest of the physical examination findings are normal.

Laboratory test results:

Calcium = 8.1 mg/dL (2.0 mmol/L)

Intact PTH = 5.0 pg/mL (5.0 ng/L)

Albumin = 4.0 mg/dL (40 g/L)

Phosphorus = 5.1 mg/dL (1.6 mmol/L)

Creatinine = 0.8 mg/dL (70.7 μmol/L)

Urinary calcium = 410 mg/24 h (10.3 mmol/d)

Which of the following is the best next step in managing her hypoparathyroidism?
options: Change calcium carbonate to calcium citrate
Increase the calcitriol dose to 1 mcg twice daily
items.explanation:
## 57 ANSWER: D) Add hydrochlorothiazide, 50 mg daily

57 A Hypoparathyroidism due to autosomal dominant hypocalcemia is most commonly caused by a heterozygous activating mutation in the gene encoding the calcium-sensing receptor (CASR). As a result, the second point for calcium sensing is reduced, thereby reducing PTH secretion from the parathyroid gland, which results in hypocalcemia and stimulation of calcuires from the kidneys. This can lead to an increase in nephrolithiasis and nephrocalcinosis and may result in chronic kidney disease. Calcium supplementation and calcitriol are the main stays of therapy. However, urinary calcium excretion may increase in response to vitamin D therapy even before normalization of serum calcium levels. Therefore, the goals of therapy are to maintain serum calcium in the normal range and to avoid hypercalciuria.

Thiazide diuretics reduce urinary calcium excretion via activation of a sodium-calcium transporter and they reduce hypercalciuria associated with hypoparathyroidism. Thus, hydrochlorothiazide (Answer D) can prove both urinary and serum calcium levels.

may be modest differences in absorption of calcium from calcium citrate compared with absorption from carbonate, but this is of little clinical import. Calcium carbonate contains a greater amount of elemental calcium than calcium citrate and hence fewer milligrams of supplement need to be taken. It should be noted that patient carbonate can have adverse effects including eructation, flatulence, and constipation when compared with associated with calcium citrate. However, because this patient is already on a stable dosage of oral calcium, there is no evidence to suggest that switching to calcium citrate (Answer A) would be beneficial.

## 57 ANSWER: D) Add hydrochlorothiazide, 50 mg daily

57 A Hypoparathyroidism due to autosomal dominant hypocalcemia is most commonly caused by a heterozygous activating mutation in the gene encoding the calcium-sensing receptor (CASR). As a result, the second point for calcium sensing is reduced, thereby reducing PTH secretion from the parathyroid gland, which results in hypocalcemia and stimulation of calcuires from the kidneys. This can lead to an increase in nephrolithiasis and nephrocalcinosis and may result in chronic kidney disease. Calcium supplementation and calcitriol are the main stays of therapy. However, urinary calcium excretion may increase in response to vitamin D therapy even before normalization of serum calcium levels. Therefore, the goals of therapy are to maintain serum calcium in the normal range and to avoid hypercalciuria.

Thiazide diuretics reduce urinary calcium excretion via activation of a sodium-calcium transporter and they reduce hypercalciuria associated with hypoparathyroidism. Thus, hydrochlorothiazide (Answer D) can prove both urinary and serum calcium levels.

However, because this patient is already on a stable dosage of oral calcium, there is no evidence to suggest that switching to calcium citrate (Answer A) would be beneficial.

You are consulted regarding a 54-year-old woman with hypocalcemia in the intensive care unit. She was hospitalized for diarrhea caused by Clostridium difficile, and she had a prolonged hospital course. She has been on total parenteral nutrition for the past month, although in the past week, she has begun to advance her oral nutrition. However, despite additional intravenous calcium supplementation, her serum calcium level has been persistently low.

On physical examination, she appears modestly ill. Her blood pressure is 105/70 mm Hg, and pulse rate is 100 beats/min. Temperature is 97.7°F (36.5°C). Weight is 137 lb (62.3 kg), and height is 64 in (162.6 cm) (BMI = 23.5 kg/m²). Mucous membranes are dry. The Chvostek sign is present. You observe no rashes or hyperpigmentation, but her skin is dry with tenting. The rest of the examination findings are normal.

Intact PTH = 20 pg/mL (20 ng/L)

Electrocardiography performed yesterday demonstrates a prolonged QT interval.

Subcutaneous injection of recombinant human PTH

Chronic diarrhea, diuretics, and proton-pump inhibitor therapy are all risk factors for hypomagnesemia. The major site of magnesium reabsorption in the kidney is at the loop of Henle, and loop diuretics result in magnesium wasting. Parenteral nutrition can also exacerbate renal magnesium wasting. Clinical manifestations of hypomagnesemia include neuromuscular excitability, electrocardiographic abnormalities, hypocalcemia. Chronic magnesium deficiency often results in PTH levels that are low or inappropriately small for the given degree of hypocalcemia, as in this patient. Severe magnesium depletion can produce PTH stance, as well as decrease PTH secretion. The mechanism of this effect is thought to be due to disinhibition of G-protein receptor, thus mimicking activation of the calcium-sensing receptor and causing inhibition of PTH. Therefore, exogenous PTH injections (Answer E) may have a blunted response.

items.question:
A 52-year-old woman is referred to you for evaluation of hypercalcemia. She describes a 4-month history of right shoulder pain. Her husband recently noticed a mass on her right scapula, which prompted further evaluation. Additionally, she describes a recent 20-lb (9.1-kg) unintentional weight loss and the development of night sweats. Medical history is otherwise unremarkable. On physical examination, her right shoulder has a limited range of motion with tenderness posterior along the scapula. Skin examination is normal without hyperpigmentation. Plain films of her right shoulder show destruction of the inferior portion of the right scapula, and MRI shows a large mass with axillary adenopathy (see image). Bone scan is remarkable for areas of focal increase in bone remodeling as seen in the left acetabulum and ilium (see image).
MRI showing a large mass with axillary adenopathy (arrow).
Axial MRI (left image) and axial CT (right image).
Laboratory test results: Serum calcium = 13.7 mg/dL (3.4 mmol/L) Albumin = 3.0 g/dL (30 g/L) Alkaline phosphatase = 760 U/L (12.7 μkat/L) Alanine aminotransferase = 77 U/L (1.28 μkat/L) Aspartate aminotransferase = 54 U/L (0.90 μkat/L) 25-Hydroxyvitamin D = 22 ng/mL (54.9 nmol/L) 1,25-Dihydroxyvitamin D = 7.0 pg/mL (18.2 pmol/L) Carbohydrate antigen 19-9 (CA19-9) = 10,858 U/mL (reference range <38 U/mL) Serum phosphorus = 2.2 mg/dL (0.71 mmol/L) Bone scan showing metastases in the left acetabulum and ilium. Bone scan showing metastases in the left acetal. Which of the following most likely mediates this patient's hypercalcemia?
items.explanation:
## 74 ANSWER: C) PTHrP

The markedly elevated carbohydrate antigen 19-9 (CA19-9) level is highly suggestive of cholangiocarcinoma, which was confirmed by FNAB of the patient's scapular mass. Cholangiocarcinoma is a rare epithelial tumor of the biliary tree, often locally advanced at the time of initial presentation and associated with high mortality. It metastasizes via the lymphatic system and less commonly spreads to bone with a predilection for the axial skeleton.

The hypercalcemia seen in cholangiocarcinoma is due to either of 2 mechanisms: tumor secretion of PTHrP or the local destruction and release of calcium from bone (which is not an answer option in this vignette). Most commonly, and as described in multiple case reports, hypercalcemia is associated with cholangiocarcinoma due to tumor PTHrP production, as was seen in this patient (thus, Answer C is correct).

PTHrP shares 70% homology with the first 13 amino acids of the aminoterminal end of PTH. Binding of PTHrP to the PTH 1 receptor simulates the major effects including osteoblastic bone resorption and distal tubular calcium reabsorption, which impairs calcium excretion but blunts proximal tubular reabsorption of phosphorus. Beyond the first 13 amino acids, the protein is unique and shows no further sequence homology with PTH. PTHrP additionally enhances malignant cell growth and survival via the secretion of tumor-activating transforming growth factor- $ \beta $ that stimulates local cancer cells.

Patients with PTHrP-mediated hypercalcemia have lower 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D₃ levels than patients with primary hyperparathyroidism given less stimulation of 1α-hydroxylase activity. PTHrP therefore would not be expected to increase intestinal absorption of calcium. The increased level of alkaline phosphatase in this patient could be the result of direct bony involvement by the tumor, PTHrP-stimulated bone turnover, or a combination of the two.

24,25-Dihydroxyvitamin D (Answer A) is an inactive metabolite of vitamin D and would not cause hypercalcemia. Ectopic PTH (Answer B) is considerably less common than PTHrP in all types of malignancy. Local factors, such as PTHrP and transforming growth factor-β, can mediate osteoclast activity and bone resorption in malignancy. Elevated fibroblast growth factor 23 levels are associated with tumor-induced osteomalacia, which is an acquired disorder of renal phosphate wasting that typically occurs with mesenchymal tumors. Tumor-induced osteomalacia is not associated with hypercalcemia (thus, Answer D is incorrect). Hypercortisolism (Answer E) is associated with bone loss for a number of reasons including diminished osteoblast activity, enhanced renal calcium excretion, and secondary hyperparathyroidism. However, hypercalcemia is not a finding in hypercortisolism. Cholangiocarcinomas have not been reported to make ACTH, ectopic production of which is seen in malignancies of the lung and other tissues. Moreover, there is no evidence of hyperpigmentation in this patient, although in malignancy-associated ectopic ACTH and Cushing syndrome, the time course may be so rapid that dermal responses to the high ACTH are not evident.

#### Educational Objective:

Describe the mechanisms of bone metastasis and PTHrP-related hypercalcemia of malignancy.

A 51-year-old man presents for evaluation of hypercalcemia. He has had progressive onset of increasing fatigue, irritability, and episodes of atypical chest pain, and initial assessment documented markedly elevated calcium levels. The patient has no family history of hypercalcemia or kidney stones. He has no personal history of kidney stones, bone pain, or joint pain. On physical examination, he is alert, oriented, and in no acute distress. Pulse rate is 60 beats/min, and blood pressure is 133/80 mm Hg. Weight is 173.5 lb (78.9 kg), and height is 72 in (182.9 cm) (BMI = 23.5 kg/m $ ^{2} $). His neck is supple with no palpable cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy. He has a large, left-sided neck mass that is freely mobile with deglutition. Neck ultrasonography is performed (see images).
LEFT NODULE TRANS The left ultrasound image measures the left nodule at 27.9 mm; the right ultrasound image measures the left nodule at 32.6 × 21.5 mm. Laboratory test results: $$ Serum~calcium=14.2mg/dL(3.6mmol/L) $$ Serum phosphorus = 2.1 mg/dL (0.68 mmol/L) $$ PTH=609\ pg/mL(609\ ng/L) $$ Serum creatinine = 2.7 mg/dL (238.7 μmol/L) $$ 25-Hydroxyvitamin D=13ng/mL(32.5nmol/L) $$ Which of the following is the best management plan?
options: Perform FNAB of the neck mass
Perform sestambi scintigraphy (technetium Tc 99 sestambi scanning)
Refer for nodule resection and neck dissection

Refer to medical oncology for adjuvant chemotherapy 83 An 18-year-old woman with 21-hydroxylase deficiency (classic congenital adrenal hyperplasia) presents to your clinic in a transition to adult care. Congenital adrenal hyperplasia was diagnosed at birth because of ambiguous genitalia and has since been treated with hydrocortisone taken at 7:30 AM and 3:30 PM, along with fludrocortisone daily. Her menstrual cycles are irregular, and she typically has 3 menses per year. She is concerned about hirsutism on her face and abdomen. She has had vaginal reconstructive surgery and is not currently interested in pregnancy.
items.explanation:
## 82 ANSWER: D) Refer to medical oncology for adjuvant chemotherapy

82 Parathyroid carcinoma is a rare cause of primary hyperparathyroidism that is seen in less than 1% to 2% of cases. Affected persons typically present in the fourth and fifth decades of life with high serum calcium and markedly elevated PTH concentrations. A palpable neck mass may be present along with overt renal and bone disease. The incidence of parathyroid carcinoma may be increasing because of earlier detection, although these are often rapidly growing. An elevated PTH level in the presence of hypercalcemia is seen in both benign primary hyperparathyroidism and parathyroid carcinoma, although PTH levels in the latter are often greater and the degree of hypercalcemia more severe. Nonetheless, the 2 conditions overlap in severity such that there is no single level of PTH or calcium that is diagnostic of either condition. Additionally, 1% of parathyroid cancers are nonfunctioning and are not identified by elevated PTH levels or symptomatic hypercalcemia and are ultimately diagnosed on the basis of a large and rapidly expanding neck mass.

Parathyroid sestamibi scintigraphy may not reliably distinguish parathyroid adenoma from carcinoma, and if parathyroid carcinoma is suspected, then a tissue diagnosis is required (thus, Answer C is incorrect). FNAB of suspected parathyroid carcinomas is associated with cutaneous spread of carcinoma along the needle tract (thus, Answer A is incorrect). Surgery is required for a definitive diagnosis, and stratification of patients at low or high risk depends on invasion into surrounding tissue or distant metastasis. Therefore, referral for nodule resection and neck dissection (Answer D) is the best management plan in this vignette. Standard parathyroidectomy does not entail evaluation of local and regional lymph nodes. In most centers, the surgical approach includes en bloc resection of the parathyroid tumor and removal of any tissue invaded by tumor. This may additionally include ipsilateral thyroidectomy and lymph node dissection with or without resection of the recurrent laryngeal nerve. Parathyroid carcinomas are difficult to treat beyond surgical cure. That is, failing total resection, most of these tumors progress and are ultimately fatal either due to the tumor metastasizing or to the sequelae of hypercalcemia. Chemotherapy (Answer E) is generally ineffective in this condition.

Calcitonin is a tumor marker essential for the diagnosis and follow-up of medullary thyroid cancer. Measuring calcitonin would be appropriate if the patient had a family history of hypercalcemia or other features that would raise suspicion of multiple endocrine neoplasia type 2A. Otherwise, a tissue diagnosis from nodule resection and neck dissection would still be the best next step (thus, Answer B is incorrect).

Interestingly, recent data suggest a genetic basis for some parathyroid carcinomas. Family members of a person with an identified CDC73 disease-causing mutation may benefit from genetic testing: absence of the mutation provides reassurance and presence of the mutation prompts a recommendation for prospective surveillance. Lifelong surveillance recommendations for persons in whom a CDC73 mutation is detected include measurement of serial calcium and PTH levels every 6 months with a low threshold for parathyroid surgery. Surveillance is recommended regardless of whether a patient with a CDC73 mutation has hypercalcemia.

##### Educational Objective:

Determine the best diagnostic and therapeutic approach to a neck mass that is most likely a parathyroid carcinoma.

A 71-year-old man is referred to your clinic for evaluation of low testosterone levels and osteoporosis. During his recent visit with his primary care physician, DXA scan was performed that showed osteoporosis of both the lumbar spine and femoral neck with T scores of -3.4 and -2.6, respectively. Workup for secondary osteoporosis was notable only for a morning total testosterone concentration of 279 ng/dL (9.68 nmol/L) (measured with mass spectrometry). His gonadotropin, prolactin, vitamin D, calcium, and TSH levels were also normal. He has been referred for the management of his bone health in light of low testosterone.

The patient has no history of fractures or back pain; however, he does mention some loss of height over the years. He reports normal libido and energy levels. His erections are weaker than before; however, this is not concerning to him. His medical history is notable for hypertension treated with lisinopril and gastroesophageal reflux disease treated with ranitidine. He has smoked half a pack of cigarettes per day for 30 years and drinks 1 glass of wine daily. He does not exercise but frequently plays with his grandchildren. His family history is remarkable for osteoporosis in his mother who had a hip fracture in her 60s. His maternal aunt also has osteoporosis.

On physical examination, pulse rate is 82 beats/min and blood pressure is 142/82 mm Hg. Weight is 168 lb (76.4 kg), and height is 67 in (170.2 cm) (BMI = 26.3 kg/m $ ^{2} $). He has kyphosis in the thoracic spine but no point tenderness. His testes are normal and his prostate is symmetrically enlarged without nodules. The rest of his examination findings are normal.

Which of the following is the best treatment option for the maintenance of this patient's bone health
items.explanation:
## 93 ANSWER: B) A bisphosphonate Oster Osteoporosis remains an under-recognized condition in men despite the fact that one-third of hip fractures that occur worldwide are in men. Furthermore, men also have a higher mortality rate than women in the first year after a hip fracture. Osteoporosis is generally a silent condition until the first fracture occurs. Height loss, however, is an important clinical manifestation of underlying osteoporosis (seen in this patient). This man has osteoporosis of both the spine and the hip. In addition to low bone density, he has other factors that add to his fracture risk: age older than 70 years, parental history of fracture, and current smoking. Hypogonadism is a known risk factor for low bone mass. In young men with pathologic hypogonadism (hypothalamic/pituitary or testicular disease) such as Kallmann syndrome, Klinefelter syndrome, or pituitary Testosterone in Older Men: Lumbar Bone Mineral Density
Adapted from Snyder PJ, Peachey H, Hannoush P, et al 1999 Effect of testosterone treatment on bone mineral density in men over 65 years of age. J Clin Endocrinol Metab 84:1966-1972.
lesions, testosterone therapy improves bone mass. The anabolic role of androgens is also evident in hyperprolactinemic men whose testosterone levels normalize after treatment with dopamine agonists. To the contrary, the effect of testosterone therapy on bone mass in older men with age-related decline in serum testosterone (which is generally mild) remains unclear. In a study of 108 men who were 65 years and older with a mean baseline serum testosterone concentration of 367 ng/dL (12.7 nmol/L), no significant increase in either lumbar spine or hip bone density was seen after 6 months of testosterone replacement vs placebo. Interestingly, a post hoc analysis of this trial based on pretreatment serum testosterone levels showed that testosterone therapy increased lumbar spine bone density by 5.9% in only those men whose pretreatment testosterone was less than 200 ng/dL (6.9 nmol/L) (Figure 1). This suggests that the only men whose bone mineral density responds to testosterone therapy are those whose baseline testosterone level is significantly low. This patient's testosterone level (279 ng/dL [9.68 nmol/L]) is only slightly below normal.
Figure 1.
A meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials that mainly enrolled older men with age-related low testosterone levels demonstrated a modest benefit in spine bone mineral density in only those men who received intramuscular testosterone, while no benefit was seen with transdermal therapy (presumably because of higher testosterone [and estradiol] levels achieved via the intramuscular route) (Figure 2A). No benefit was seen in hip bone mineral density (Figure 2B). Even more importantly, there are no data showing that testosterone therapy reduces fracture rates. Therefore, the Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guidelines for Male Osteoporosis recommend that in men who are at high risk for fracture, an agent with proven antifracture effectiveness be initiated, such as a bisphosphonate (Answer B) or teriparatide. In fact, the addition of such an agent is recommended even in those patients at high risk who might already be on testosterone therapy. The use of testosterone therapy in lieu of a “bone drug” is only recommended in men who are at borderline-high risk of fracture and have testosterone levels below 200 ng/dL (6.9 nmol/L) along with symptoms of hypogonadism or in men with contraindications to approved pharmacologic agents for osteoporosis. Alendronate, zoledronic acid, and risedronate have been shown to possess antifracture efficacy, while fracture data on ibandronate are not available.

Furthermore, men also have a higher mortality rate than women in the first year after a hip fracture. Osteoporosis is generally a silent condition until the first fracture occurs. Height loss, however, is an important clinical manifestation of underlying osteoporosis (seen in this patient). This man has osteoporosis of both the spine and the hip. In addition to low bone density, he has other factors that add to his fracture risk: age older than 70 years, parental history of fracture, and current smoking. Hypogonadism is a known risk factor for low bone mass. A 64-year-old man is being very old. He has had a compression fracture at T10. He has had 94 lasted for about 2 months when he was in his late 50s. His medical history is notable for gastroesophageal reflux disease with a recent duodenal ulcer found on esophagogastroduodenoscopy. His medications include omeprazole and a daily multivitamin that contains 500 mg of calcium. He has an 8-oz glass of milk and a cup of yogurt every day for breakfast. He has never smoked cigarettes. He drinks 1 glass of wine with dinner. He has never had kidney stones. He has lost about 1.5 in (3.8 cm) from his peak adult height. He does not appear cushingoid, and his examination height is 70 in (177.8 cm) and weight is 180 lb (81.8 kg) (BMI = 25.8 kg/m²).

On physical examination, height is 70 in (177.8 cm) and weight is 180 lb (81.8 kg) (BMI = 25.8 kg/m $ ^{2} $). Ble pressure is 135/78 mm Hg, and pulse rate is 72 beats/min. He does not appear cushingoid, and his examination findings are normal.

Laboratory test results:

Calcium = 9.5 mg/dL (2.4 mmol/L)

Intact PTH = 42 pg/mL (42 ng/L)

25-Hydroxyvitamin D = 33 ng/mL (82.4 nmol/L)

Creatinine = 1.0 mg/dL (88.4 μmol/L)

Alkaline phosphatase = 88 U/L (1.5 μkat/L)

Complete blood count, testosterone, TSH, serum and urine protein

electrophoresis, and 24-hour urine free cortisol are normal

24-hour urinary calcium = 150 mg/24 h (3.8 mmol/d)

Bone mineral density DXA scan reveals an L1 through L4 T score of -1.9 and a femoral neck T score of -1.6. FRAX 10-year probabilities of a major osteoporotic fracture and a hip fracture are 12% and 2.3%, respectively.

Which of the following treatments is the best recommendation?
items.explanation:
## 94 ANSWER: B) Intravenous zoledronic acid

Vertebral compression fractures are a risk factor for subsequent vertebral fractures, as well as other osteoporotic fractures. They are also associated with significant morbidity, including chronic back pain, restricted lung function, reduced mobility, and decreased quality of life. Unfortunately, most patients with fragility fractures are not treated for osteoporosis, despite evidence that treatment is beneficial.

Patients who present with vertebral fractures should be worked up for secondary causes of osteoporosis. This patient had a full secondary osteoporosis evaluation, which was unrevealing. Of interest, there is a potential connection between proton-pump inhibitors and bone loss, such as is seen in this patient. Although the exact mechanism remains unproven, the increase in gastric pH reduces the absorption of calcium and thereby results in one loss when these medications are used long term.

This vignette presents a common scenario in which an asymptomatic vertebral compression fracture is noted on imaging obtained for other reasons. It is the clinician's role to recognize these events as skeletal failure that requires prompt evaluation and treatment. This patient's atraumatic spinal compression fracture confirms the diagnosis of osteoporosis, despite the fact that his bone mineral density does not meet the T-score definition of osteoporosis (-2.5 or less). The National Osteoporosis Foundation recommends the following: (1) initiating pharmacologic treatment in patients with hip or vertebral fractures (clinical or asymptomatic) regardless of T-score; (2) initiating therapy in patients with T scores of -2.5 or less at the femoral neck, total hip, or lumbar spine by OXA after appropriate evaluation; and (3) initiating therapy in postmenopausal women and men 50 years or older who have low bone mineral density (T score between -1.0 and -2.5 [osteopenia]) at the femoral neck, total hip, or lumbar spine by DXA and 10-year probabilities of hip fracture or major osteoporotic fracture of ≥3% and ≥20%, respectively (based on the US-adapted WHO absolute fracture risk model [FRAX; www.NOF.org and www.shefac.uk/FRAX]). Although the FRAX score in this patient is under the threshold for treatment, as it only accounts for femoral neck T score, on the basis of his recent vertebral fracture, treatment is warranted.

Oral bisphosphonates (such as alendronate and risedronate) and zoledronic acid are good options for first-line therapy because they reduce the risk of hip and spine fracture. Although oral alendronate (Answer A) would be a treatment option in this patient, given his recent duodenal ulcer and esophageal reflux disease, it may not be the best choice. Gastroesophageal disease is not an absolute contraindication to use of oral bisphosphonates, but caution should be used in patient with active upper gastrointestinal problems such as Barrett esophagus, dysphagia, other esophageal diseases, gastritis, duodenitis, or ulcers. Intravenous zoledronic acid (Answer B) confers no gastrointestinal risk for this patient and therefore would be the preferred choice.

Calcitonin (Answer D) reduces risk of spine fracture and may offer analgesic effects in some patients with back pain related to acute fracture; however, it is less potent than other available options and this patient is asymptomatic. Although results from randomized, placebo-controlled trials have suggested little or no benefit in patients with acute vertebral compression fracture, kyphoplasty (Answer C) (the installation of bone cement after inflation of the vertebral body) has been used in patients with severe, incapacitating pain related to the fracture expansion of the vertebral body. These procedures also have been shown to provide clinical pain relief in when pain management has failed. items.question:
A 45-year-old woman presents with a 6-month history of bone pain that is most severe in her shins. She describes the pain as dull, aching, and worse with activity. She has also noted fatigue and muscle weakness that makes rising from seated or supine positions difficult. She has noticed that these symptoms of weakness often occur after eating a meal. She underwent Roux-en-Y gastric bypass surgery 2 years ago. Before her bariatric operation, she had type 2 diabetes that was managed with insulin glargine and lispro. She also had a history of hyperlipidemia, and preoperative testing revealed elevated liver enzymes that were attributed to fatty liver. Since her operation, she has successfully lost 85 lb (38.6 kg). She no longer requires insulin for management of her diabetes. She currently does not take any medications or over-the-counter vitamins or supplements.

On physical examination, weight is 190 lb (86.4 kg) and height is 64 in (162.6 cm) (BMI = 32.6 kg/m²). Blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg, and heart rate is 72 beats/min. Findings on thyroid examination are normal. There are well-healed surgical scars on her abdomen. She has no kyphosis or tenderness to spinal palpation. She has difficulty standing from a seated position. She has tenderness over her tibial surfaces. Reflexes are 2+ throughout and the Chvostek sign is negative. The rest of her examination findings are normal.

Fasting laboratory test results:

Serum calcium = 8.9 mg/dL (2.2 mmol/L)

Phosphate = 1.9 mg/dL (0.6 mmol/L)

Albumin = 3.7 g/dL (37 g/L)

PTH = 123 pg/mL (123 ng/L)

Creatinine = 0.9 mg/dL (79.6 μmol/L)

Alkaline phosphatase = 250 U/L (4.2  $ \mu $kat/L)

Which of the following would assist in the diagnosis?
items.explanation:
## 100 ANSWER: B) Measurement of 25-hydroxyvitamin D

Obesity is associated with vitamin D deficiency due to reduced bioavailability from sequestration in fat. Patients who undergo bariatric surgery are at increased risk for malabsorption of vitamin D, leading to further nutritional deficits because of the loss of sufficient gastrointestinal absorptive surface. Deficiency in 25-hydroxyvitamin D results in decreased calcium and phosphorous absorption from the gut and increased PTH, which leads to increased bone resorption to maintain serum calcium in the normal range. Severe and prolonged vitamin D deficiency results in osteomalacia or a mineralization defect in bone where osteoid remains undermineralized, soft, and vulnerable to fracture. Therefore, measurement of 25-hydroxyvitamin D (Answer B) is the best diagnostic step now.

Osteomalacia is characterized by deficient bone mineralization, and in combination with hyperparathyroidism, it can lead to an increase in bone loss and fractures. Affected patients often present with bone pain and proximal muscle weakness, which is aggravated by physical activity. Levels of 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D are often normal, and its measurement (Answer A) is therefore not helpful in making the diagnosis. Patients with osteomalacia may present with reduced bone density on radiographic studies, with thinning of the cortex, and possible cortical stress fractures, often called Looser zones or pseudofractures. Bone density scans (Answer C) may show generalized osteopenia; however, this finding is nonspecific and does not distinguish between osteomalacia and osteoporosis. An iliac crest biopsy (Answer E) is not needed either, as it would reveal osteomalacia as well, without identifying a cause that could be elucidated more rapidly, simply, and less expensively with standard laboratory studies. Obtaining a bone scan (Answer D) to further evaluate her bone pain might be indicated in certain settings, such as in metastatic cancer or Paget disease. In the setting of high bone turnover, as would be the case in this vignette, bone scan will reveal a diffusely high uptake throughout the skeleton, including the tibia. Thus, it would not shed light on the correct diagnosis.

#### Educational Objective:

Evaluate for osteomalacia in a patient with risk factors for vitamin D deficiency.

UpToDate Topic Review(s): Clinical manifestations, diagnosis, and treatment of osteomalacia

##### Reference(s):

Johnson JM, Maher JW, DeMaria EJ, Downs RW, Wolfe LG, Kellum JM. The long-term effects of gastric bypass on vitamin D metabolism. Ann Surg. 2006;243(5):701-704.

Service GJ, Thompson GB, Service FJ, Andrews JC, Collazo-Clavell ML, Lloyd RV. Hyperinsulinemic hypoglycemia with nesidioblastosis after gastric-bypass surgery. N Engl J Med. 2005;353(3):249-254.

items.question:
A 69-year-old man comes to you for evaluation of osteoporosis. He had a hip fracture 6 months ago and has since been treated with risedronate, 35 mg orally once weekly. His initial DXA scan shortly after his fracture, revealed the following T scores: lumbar spine, -1.9, femoral neck, -2.8, and total hip -2.6. He reports epigastric discomfort when he takes the risedronate, despite taking it on an empty stomach with a full glass of water and waiting at least 30 minutes before eating food or taking other medications. A trial of a proton-pump inhibitor has not improved his symptoms.

He has been taking cholecalciferol, 2000 IU daily, in addition to calcium carbonate, 600 mg twice daily with meals.

Laboratory test results:

Serum creatinine = 1.9 mg/dL (168.0 μmol/L)

PTH = 32 pg/mL (32 ng/L)

Estimated glomerular filtration rate = 30 mL/min

25-Hydroxyvitamin D = 38 ng/mL (94.8 nmol/L)

Serum calcium = 10.0 mg/dL (2.5 mmol/L)

Total testosterone = 390 ng/dL (13.5 nmol/L)

Which of the following should be your next step?
options: Discontinue risedronate and start monthly oral ibandronate
Discontinue risedronate and start intravenous zoledronic acid
items.explanation:
## 111 ANSWER: E) Discontinue risedronate and start denosumab

Bisphosphonates are poorly absorbed orally, with less than 1% absorbed in the gastrointestinal tract. In addition, gastrointestinal adverse effects, including gastritis and esophagitis are relatively common. Often, these symptoms resolve within a few weeks or within a few doses. However, an alternative medication should be prescribed when gastrointestinal symptoms persist. Therefore, continuing the current medication (Answer A) is incorrect. Prescribing another oral bisphosphonate (Answer C) with a similar adverse effect profile when the patient has developed gastrointestinal adverse effects is not a tenable long-term solution. Moreover, ibandronate does not offer fracture protection at the hip. In this patient, intravenous zoledronic acid (Answer D) is desirable because in a trial of zoledronic acid for secondary hip fracture prevention, a mortality benefit was documented and the intravenous administration of the agent solves the issue of gastrointestinal intolerance; however, this patient's reduced renal function is a contraindication to use of zoledronic acid.

Given his history of hip fracture and low bone density in the osteoporotic range, he should receive treatment. Alternative therapies include calcitonin, teriparatide, or denosumab. In this patient, replacing risedronate with calcitonin (Answer B), which does not offer fracture protection at the hip, is not a good choice. Teriparatide (not an option offered in this vignette) is indicated to increase bone mass in men with primary or hypogonadal osteoporosis at high risk for fracture. Thus, it would be an option for this man. Denosumab (Answer E) given by injection once every 6 months, reduces the risk of fracture at both the hip and spine, as demonstrated in the FREEDOM trial. Because it is given by subcutaneous injection, it avoids the gastrointestinal adverse effects common to bisphosphonates and it can be used in the setting of chronic kidney disease.

### Educational Objective:

Educational Objectives: Prioritize the choice of osteoporosis therapy on the basis of patient characteristics and contraindications.

ToDate Topic Review(s):

Treatment of osteoporosis in men

items.question:
A 50-year-old man is referred for evaluation of hypercalcemia. He presented to the emergency department with concerns of fever, dyspnea, muscle weakness, fatigue, anorexia, and constipation. His symptoms had started a few weeks earlier and had gradually worsened. He has a history of HIV infection, which had been managed with antiretroviral medications in the past. However, he stopped taking his medication more than 6 months ago.

On physical examination, the patient appears to be in respiratory distress. Respiratory rate is 30 breaths/min, with an oxygen saturation of 93% while he is at rest and breathing room air. Pulse rate is 85 beats/min, blood pressure is 135/80 mm Hg, and temperature is 101.1°F (38.4°C). Weight is 165 lb (75 kg), and height is 68.5 in (cm) (BMI = 24.7 kg/m²). The heart sounds are normal, and lung auscultation reveals bilateral crackles at the base radiograph demonstrates bilateral basilar infiltrates.

Chest radiograph demonstrates bilateral basilar in

Laboratory test results:

White blood cell count = 11,300/μL (11.3 × 10⁹/L)

Creatinine = 1.8 mg/dL (159.1 μmol/L)

Alkaline phosphatase = 100 U/L (1.7  $ \mu $kat/L)

After aggressive intravenous hydration, which of the following is the best next step in the management of his hypercalcemia?
items.explanation:
## 117 ANSWER: D) Prednisone The critical element in this

The critical element in this vignette is that the patient does not have elevated PTH levels. His hypercalcemia is secondary to extrarenal conversion to 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D by activated macrophages, rather than primary hyperparathyroidism in which parathyroid autonomy leads to hypercalcemia and elevated PTH. Ectopic activation of vitamin D causes absorptive hypercalcemia and subsequently a suppressed PTH level. Phosphate absorption, like calcium absorption, tends to be increased (and because of hypoparathyroidism, urinary phosphate excretion is reduced), such that the serum calcium-phosphate product is elevated and ectopic calcification and nephrocalcinosis are relatively common.

Pneumocystis jirovecii (formerly Pneumocystis carinii) is an opportunistic infection associated with HIV infection and organ transplant. Hypercalcemia associated with P. jirovecii pneumonia can be severe, and it is due to the extrarenal conversion of 25-hydroxyvitamin D to 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D. The list of granulomatous disorders associated with HIV infection is extensive and includes tuberculosis, mycobacterium avium, histoplasmosis, and P. jirovecii. This patient has multiple manifestations of P. jirovecii pneumonia including fever, dyspnea, and fatigue. In addition, he has tachypnea, low oxygen saturation, and a chest radiograph demonstrating the presence of pneumonia. His CD₄ count is low, and he is not taking antiretroviral agents or P. jirovecii prophylaxis, thus putting him at increased risk for this opportunistic infection. In addition, he has symptomatic hypercalcemia (symptoms include fatigue, anorexia, and constipation).

Appropriate treatment of hypercalcemia in a patient with granulomatous disease and ectopic activation of vitamin D can generally be accomplished by aggressive fluid hydration and administration of glucocorticoids, such as prednisone (thus, Answer D is correct). The use of glucocorticoids is safe and effective in this setting. In addition, once a specific etiology for the granulomatous disease has been confirmed, P. jirovecii in this case, treatment directed at that pathogen (eg, intravenous trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole) should be initiated.

Because the basis for the hypercalcemia is excess absorption of calcium from the gut, which in turn lowers PTH, bone turnover in these individuals is often very low. Subcutaneous calcitonin works to decrease serum calcium levels by inhibiting osteoclastic bone resorption, and to a much lesser extent by increasing renal calcium excretion. Thus, the mechanism of action limits the benefit of calcitonin (Answer E).

Zoledronic acid (Answer B) effectively lowers serum calcium when excessive bone resorption is the cause of hypercalcemia. Similarly, denosumab (Answer C) lowers serum calcium in high bone turnover states, but would not reduce the conversion of 25-hydroxyvitamin D to 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D. Neither agent would therefore be useful in this setting.

Cinacalcet (Answer A) can lower calcium levels in patients with hyperparathyroidism because it sensitizes the parathyroid cells to extracellular calcium. In this patient who has a suppressed PTH level, it would be of no benefit.

##### Educational Objective:

Diagnose hypercalcemia due to granulomatous disease and recommend appropriate treatment.

items.question: A postmenopausal woman with estrogen-receptor-positive breast cancer is about to start an aromatase inhibitor after surgery. DXA shows low bone density. Which therapy best prevents AI-induced bone loss?
items.explanation: Aromatase inhibitors accelerate bone loss; denosumab (anti-RANKL) is approved to prevent AI-induced bone loss when T-score ≤ −2.0 or multiple risk factors.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 23: "Prevent bone loss in a patient starting aromatase inhibitor therapy for breast cancer."

items.title: Why answer C is correct
items.text: Aromatase inhibitors accelerate bone loss; denosumab (anti-RANKL) is approved to prevent AI-induced bone loss when T-score ≤ −2.0 or multiple risk factors.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 23: "Prevent bone loss in a patient starting aromatase inhibitor therapy for breast cancer."

items.title: How to apply this case clinically
items.text: Offer bone-directed therapy when starting aromatase inhibitors in postmenopausal women at elevated fracture risk—do not rely on calcium/vitamin D alone.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 23: "Prevent bone loss in a patient starting aromatase inhibitor therapy for breast cancer."

items.question: A 56-year-old woman has weight loss, confusion, hypercalcemia (14.7 mg/dL), splenomegaly with infiltrative masses on CT, and normal alkaline phosphatase. Which laboratory pattern is most characteristic?
options: High urinary calcium; low PTH; low PTHrP
Low urinary calcium; high PTH; low PTHrP
High urinary calcium; high PTH; low PTHrP
High urinary calcium; low PTH; high PTHrP
Low urinary calcium; low PTH; high PTHrP
items.explanation: B-cell lymphoma can produce extrarenal 1,25-(OH)₂D via macrophage 1α-hydroxylase, causing absorptive hypercalcemia with suppressed PTH and PTHrP.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 66: "Recognize calcitriol-mediated hypercalcemia in lymphoproliferative disease."

items.title: How to apply this case clinically
items.text: In lymphoma-associated hypercalcemia, measure 1,25-(OH)₂D; pattern differs from PTHrP-mediated humoral hypercalcemia of malignancy.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 66: "Recognize calcitriol-mediated hypercalcemia in lymphoproliferative disease."

items.statement: Denosumab prevents aromatase inhibitor–induced bone loss in high-risk postmenopausal women.
items.explanation: RANKL inhibition reduces bone resorption during estrogen deprivation.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 23: "Denosumab increases the mineral density in women undergoing treatment with aromatase inhibitors"

items.explanation: Hypercalcemia feedback suppresses PTH; PTHrP is typically low.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 66: "suppressed PTH (associated with low PTHrP levels)"

items.statement: Distal radius BMD may improve more than spine after parathyroidectomy in PHPT.
items.explanation: Cortical bone sites may show greater gains.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 17: "Distal radius bone mineral density"

items.statement: Hypomagnesemia must be corrected before hypocalcemia responds to calcium/PTH therapy.
items.explanation: Magnesium depletion impairs PTH secretion and action.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 71: "magnesium deficiency must be corrected first"

items.statement: Teriparatide is first-line for severe postmenopausal osteoporosis with multiple fractures.
items.explanation: Antiresorptives or anabolic agents per guidelines; context-dependent—not always teriparatide first.
items.reference: ESAP 2015: "Osteoporosis"

items.statement: 24-hour urinary calcium helps distinguish FHH from PHPT.
items.explanation: Low urinary calcium with hypercalcemia suggests FHH.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 45: "Measure 24-hour urinary calcium"

items.statement: Vitamin D repletion is unnecessary when starting osteoporosis therapy.
items.explanation: Correct deficiency before/during antiresorptive therapy.
items.reference: ESAP 2015: "vitamin D"

items.statement: Subtotal parathyroidectomy is appropriate for 4-gland hyperplasia in renal transplant hyperparathyroidism.
items.explanation: Multigland disease in tertiary HPT often needs subtotal resection.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 6: "Subtotal parathyroidectomy"

items.assertion: Tamoxifen combined with an aromatase inhibitor is standard for bone protection on AI therapy.
items.reason: Combination is not standard; denosumab or bisphosphonates are used.

items.reason: Many lymphomas cause calcitriol-mediated hypercalcemia with low PTHrP.

items.reason: PTH response is blunted until magnesium is replete.

items.reason: Hypertension may improve but is not guaranteed to resolve.
items.explanation: A false.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 17: "Hypertension"

items.assertion: FHH presents with markedly elevated PTH and hypercalcemia.
items.reason: FHH typically has mild hypercalcemia with normal or mildly elevated PTH and low urine Ca.
items.explanation: A false.
items.reference: ESAP 2015: "Familial hypocalciuric hypercalcemia"

items.assertion: Calcitonin is first-line for AI-induced bone loss.
items.explanation: A false.
items.reference: ESAP 2015 Case 23: "Calcitonin is a weak antiresorptive agent"

items.assertion: Hypercalcemia in granulomatous disease responds to glucocorticoids.
items.reason: Steroids reduce calcitriol production in macrophage-driven hypercalcemia.
items.explanation: Both true and linked.
